 {"id":"bd-1rzr","title":"Action Timeline: Alert Threshold Calibration (Conformal + e-process)","description":"## Goal
Set change-point alert thresholds with formal false-positive control and provide an explanation ledger per alert.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Conformal thresholding on residuals from the sketch baseline:
- Given calibration residuals R = {r_1..r_n}, threshold q = quantile_{1-alpha} using (n+1) rule.
- Alert when r_t > q.

Anytime-valid layer:
- Maintain an e-process on residuals to allow early alerts without inflating false positives.
- Example: e_t = exp(lambda * (r_t - mu) - (lambda^2 * sigma^2)/2), E_t = product e_t.
- Trigger if E_t > 1/alpha.

## Acceptance Criteria
- FPR <= alpha on synthetic and recorded traces.
- Alert carries evidence ledger: residual, q, conformal score, e-value, alpha.
- No alert spam under steady-state traffic.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_conformal_threshold: threshold equals (1-alpha) quantile of calibration set.
- unit_evalue_monotone: e-process nonnegative and grows with evidence.
- property_fpr_control: empirical FPR <= alpha + tolerance.

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Replay trace with known change points; log alert timing and evidence.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-1ti43","title":"Replace guarded .unwrap() with contextual .expect() in production code","description":"Replace bare .unwrap() calls in production code paths with .expect() messages that document the invariant being relied upon. These are all guarded unwraps (safe due to preceding checks) but lack context for debugging if the invariant is ever violated.

Scope (production code only, not tests):
- ftui-render/src/budget.rs:1047 - controller.as_mut().unwrap() guarded by is_some()
- ftui-render/src/buffer.rs:1093 - scissor_stack.last().unwrap() invariant: always ≥1 element
- ftui-render/src/buffer.rs:1127 - opacity_stack.last().unwrap() invariant: always ≥1 element  
- ftui-render/src/presenter.rs:914 - cursor_x.unwrap() guarded by forward check
- ftui-runtime/src/program.rs:317,328 - into_iter().next().unwrap() guarded by len==1
- ftui-runtime/src/queueing_scheduler.rs:1148 - current_job.take().unwrap()
- ftui-runtime/src/resize_coalescer.rs:1192 - event_times.front().unwrap()
- ftui-runtime/src/simulator.rs:155 - frames.last().unwrap()
- ftui-core/src/hover_stabilizer.rs:176 - current_target.unwrap()

Each .unwrap() → .expect("invariant: ...") with a message describing why it's safe.

Validation: cargo check --all-targets && cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check && cargo test","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T17:57:36.641137112Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T18:02:21.862221384Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T18:02:21.862197359Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-376","title":"Implement interactive widgets (List, Scrollbar, Input)","description":"PHASE 5 - INTERACTIVE WIDGETS

## Background
Interactive widgets handle user input and state changes. List provides selectable items, Scrollbar visualizes scroll position, Input accepts text entry.

## Technical Requirements

### List Widget
- Items with optional styling per item
- Single/multi selection modes
- Keyboard navigation (up/down/pageup/pagedown/home/end)
- Highlight style for selected item
- Scroll into view on selection change
- Item filtering/search support

### Scrollbar Widget
- Vertical and horizontal orientation
- Track, thumb, and arrow styles
- Thumb size proportional to content ratio
- Click/drag support (if mouse enabled)
- Auto-hide when content fits

### Input Widget
- Single-line text input
- Cursor positioning with blinking
- Text selection (shift+arrow)
- Clipboard operations (copy/paste)
- Placeholder text when empty
- Password mode (masked input)
- Input validation hooks

## Design Rationale
These widgets encapsulate common interaction patterns. State management follows Elm architecture - widgets emit events, parent handles state updates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] List with selection modes
- [ ] List keyboard navigation
- [ ] Scrollbar sizing algorithm
- [ ] Scrollbar orientation support
- [ ] Input cursor management
- [ ] Input text selection
- [ ] All widgets implement StatefulWidget trait
- [ ] Event emission for state changes

## StatefulWidget Trait
pub trait StatefulWidget {
    type State;
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer, state: &mut Self::State);
}

 {"id":"bd-37xrr","title":"Mermaid Init Directives + Theme/Config Bridging","description":"Background
- Mermaid supports %%{init}%% directives to set theme, direction, and configuration.
- We need a deterministic bridge that maps init directives into the TME config + style system without allowing arbitrary side effects.

Scope
- Parse %%{init: { ... }}%% blocks into a structured InitConfig (subset of Mermaid schema).
- Map supported keys to TME config + theme tokens (e.g., theme, themeVariables, flowchart.direction).
- Validate and clamp values; unsupported keys produce deterministic warnings (compat matrix).
- Ensure init directives are applied before style resolution and layout.

Considerations
- Must be deterministic and safe: no network, no JS, no side effects.
- Must not allow init to bypass engine guardrails (max_nodes, budgets).

Tests / Validation
- Unit tests for parsing supported init keys and rejecting invalid values.
- Snapshot tests showing themeVariable changes reflected in diagrams.
- PTY E2E logs include init_config_hash + warnings.

Success Criteria
- Supported init directives influence layout/style deterministically.
- Unsupported init keys produce stable warnings and do not crash.","notes":"Added Mermaid init directive bridging helpers in crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid.rs: MermaidThemeOverrides + MermaidInitConfig::theme_overrides, apply_init_directives() applies flowchart direction to AST, plus init checksum helpers (FNV-1a) and MermaidPrepared pipeline (prepare/prepare_with_policy) that applies init directives before validation, computes init_config_hash, and emits optional JSONL (log_path). Added tests for prepare init override + checksum change. Quality gates: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings pass.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CloudyElk","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:05:21.486242707Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T05:13:39.787454404Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T05:07:17.238466371Z","close_reason":"Init directive parsing + theme overrides + flowchart direction applied; tests in place; docs warning taxonomy aligned","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-37xrr","depends_on_id":"bd-315zw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:05:31.483581129Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-37xrr","depends_on_id":"bd-3monr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:05:31.718864046Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-37xrr","depends_on_id":"bd-pt0dv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:05:31.247968762Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-381","title":"Implement syntax highlighting for code blocks","description":"PHASE 7 - EXTRAS: SYNTAX HIGHLIGHTING

## Background
Syntax highlighting is essential for code-centric TUI applications. Feature-gated as optional extra.

## Technical Requirements

### Highlighting Engine
- Use syntect or tree-sitter for parsing
- Support common languages: Rust, Python, TypeScript, JSON, YAML, TOML, Markdown
- Theme integration (VS Code themes, base16, etc.)
- Line number gutter option
- Diff highlighting (additions, deletions)

### API Design

pub struct SyntaxHighlighter {
    syntax_set: SyntaxSet,
    theme_set: ThemeSet,
    current_theme: String,
}

impl SyntaxHighlighter {
    pub fn new() -> Self;
    
    /// Highlight code string
    pub fn highlight(&self, code: &str, lang: &str) -> Text;
    
    /// Highlight with line numbers
    pub fn highlight_numbered(&self, code: &str, lang: &str, start_line: usize) -> Text;
    
    /// Set theme
    pub fn set_theme(&mut self, name: &str);
    
    /// Get available themes
    pub fn themes(&self) -> &[String];
}

### Performance Considerations
- Lazy syntax loading
- Incremental re-highlighting for edits
- Cache parsed syntax per file extension
- Background highlighting for large files

## Tests Required
- [ ] Rust code highlights correctly
- [ ] Unknown language falls back gracefully
- [ ] Theme switching works
- [ ] Line numbers align correctly
- [ ] Diff highlighting marks changes

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 10+ languages supported
- [ ] 3+ built-in themes
- [ ] Integration with Paragraph widget
- [ ] Performance: < 10ms for 1000 lines

Implement offset clamp based on available height (including variable row heights in Table).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"IndigoBeacon","created_at":"2026-02-07T23:36:46.032593526Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T00:08:28.749701480Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T00:07:19.614104558Z","close_reason":"Completed: List/Table clamp offset to fill viewport on resize; added unit + integration tests; gates pass","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1003,"issue_id":"bd-387rr","author":"IndigoBeacon","text":"Implemented resize-safe scroll offset clamping so List/Table keep the bottom visible and fill newly-grown viewports.\n\n- List: clamp offset to max_offset = items.len() - viewport_height (saturating).\n- Table: compute bottom_offset by scanning rows from bottom using row.height (+ bottom_margin except last row), then clamp offset to bottom_offset.\n- Added regression tests in-place:\n  - list_clamps_offset_to_fill_viewport_on_resize\n  - table_clamps_offset_to_fill_viewport_on_resize\n  - table_clamps_offset_to_fill_viewport_with_variable_row_heights\n\nQuality gates: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test all pass.","created_at":"2026-02-08T00:08:28Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-389n","title":"Implement FrameBudgetConfig with sensible defaults","description":"# Implement FrameBudgetConfig with sensible defaults

## Background
Part of Render Budget Enforcement (bd-1iqn). Configuration struct for tuning budget behavior per-application.

## Technical Design

```rust
/// Frame budget configuration
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct FrameBudgetConfig {
    /// Total frame budget (default: 16ms for ~60fps feel)
    pub total: Duration,
    
    /// Budget reserved for diff computation
    pub diff_budget: Duration,
    
    /// Budget reserved for ANSI emission
    pub present_budget: Duration,
    
    /// Whether to skip frames when severely over budget
    pub allow_frame_skip: bool,
    
    /// Minimum frames between degradation level increases
    /// Prevents rapid oscillation
    pub degradation_cooldown: u32,
    
    /// Minimum frames at a level before upgrading
    /// Ensures stability before returning to higher fidelity
    pub upgrade_threshold: u32,
    
    /// Whether to log budget overruns
    pub log_overruns: bool,
    
    /// Show visual indicator when degraded (helpful for debugging)
    /// When enabled, shows small icon in corner during degradation
    pub show_degradation_indicator: bool,
    
    /// Callback for degradation events (for telemetry/monitoring)
    pub on_degradation: Option<Box<dyn Fn(DegradationLevel, DegradationLevel) + Send + Sync>>,
    
    /// Cost estimates for common operations
    pub cost_estimates: CostEstimates,
}

/// Estimated costs for operations (for should_degrade checks)
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct CostEstimates {
    /// Cost of Unicode box-drawing borders
    pub unicode_border: Duration,
    /// Cost of styled text rendering  
    pub styled_text: Duration,
    /// Cost of animation tick
    pub animation_tick: Duration,
}

impl Default for FrameBudgetConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            total: Duration::from_millis(16),
            diff_budget: Duration::from_millis(2),
            present_budget: Duration::from_millis(4),
            allow_frame_skip: true,
            degradation_cooldown: 3,
            upgrade_threshold: 5,
            log_overruns: true,
            show_degradation_indicator: false,  // Off by default in production
            on_degradation: None,
            cost_estimates: CostEstimates::default(),
        }
    }
}

impl FrameBudgetConfig {
    /// Create config for high-performance mode (8ms budget)
    pub fn high_performance() -> Self {
        Self {
            total: Duration::from_millis(8),
            degradation_cooldown: 2,
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
    
    /// Create config for low-latency mode (aggressive degradation)
    pub fn low_latency() -> Self {
        Self {
            total: Duration::from_millis(8),
            degradation_cooldown: 1,
            upgrade_threshold: 10,
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
    
    /// Create config for development (shows indicator, verbose logging)
    pub fn development() -> Self {
        Self {
            show_degradation_indicator: true,
            log_overruns: true,
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
}
```

## User Experience Features

### Degradation Indicator
When `show_degradation_indicator: true`, a small icon appears in the corner:
- `[!]` when in SimpleBorders or NoStyling
- `[!!]` when in EssentialOnly or Skeleton
- Hidden when at Full quality

### Degradation Callback
For monitoring/telemetry, applications can register a callback:
```rust
config.on_degradation = Some(Box::new(|old, new| {
    metrics::counter!("render.degradation").increment(1);
}));
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All config fields with sensible defaults
- [ ] Preset configurations (high_performance, low_latency, development)
- [ ] Optional degradation indicator
- [ ] Optional callback for degradation events
- [ ] All fields documented

 {"id":"bd-38d17","title":"Diagram Minimap for Large Graph Navigation","description":"## Goal
For diagrams with 20+ nodes, render a small minimap (8-12 cells tall) in a corner showing the entire diagram at tiny scale using Braille dots. A rectangular viewport indicator shows which portion is currently visible.

## Why
- Large diagrams are the primary case where terminal rendering struggles — 80×24 shows maybe 8-10 nodes at readable size.
- Without a minimap, users lose spatial orientation when panned/zoomed into a large graph.
- Minimaps are a well-understood UX pattern from code editors, games, and CAD tools.
- The Canvas/Braille system is perfect: at 2×4 dots per cell, a 20×8 minimap covers 40×32 "pixels" — enough to show topology of a 50-node graph.

## Implementation
1. Compute minimap scale: full diagram bounds / minimap area dimensions.
2. Render nodes as filled Braille dots (small filled rectangles at minimap scale).
3. Render edges as Braille lines between node centers.
4. Overlay viewport rectangle (highlighted border) showing currently visible region.
5. Position minimap in configurable corner (default: bottom-right).
6. Toggle with 'm' key.
7. Click on minimap to jump viewport to that position (if mouse support available).

## Design Decisions
- Minimap always uses Braille mode (even if main diagram uses cell mode) for maximum information density.
- Node labels are NOT shown in minimap — just dots for topology.
- Selected node (from navigation feature) highlighted in minimap with accent color.
- Minimap has a subtle border/background to distinguish from main diagram.

## Dependencies
- Canvas rendering infrastructure (bd-ukp1f)
- Viewport/zoom math (already partially in MermaidShowcase)

## Files to Modify
- crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_render.rs (add render_minimap method)
- crates/ftui-demo-showcase/src/screens/mermaid_showcase.rs (minimap toggle + positioning)

## Tests
- Unit: minimap scale calculation for various diagram sizes
- Snapshot: 20-node diagram with minimap visible at 120×40
- Unit: viewport indicator position matches actual viewport","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"EmeraldBasin","created_at":"2026-02-05T15:28:01.638550157Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T01:14:30.711174017Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T01:14:30.711145924Z","close_reason":"Completed: Braille minimap overlay with 11 tests, integrated into showcase","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["canvas","interactivity","mermaid"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-38d17","depends_on_id":"bd-ukp1f.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T15:30:16.286279635Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
